description: Attention, positional encoding, mixture-of-experts, architectural trade-offs.

questions.question: Which 2017 paper introduced the Transformer architecture that underpins modern LLMs?
options: 'ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks'
'Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning'
'Attention Is All You Need'
'Language Models are Few-Shot Learners'
questions.explanation: 'Attention Is All You Need' (Vaswani et al., 2017, Google) introduced the Transformer, replacing recurrence with self-attention and enabling today's LLMs.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of 'attention' in Transformer models?
options: To analyze, categorize, and adapt the model's conversational tone to match the user's current emotional state in real-time
To dynamically weight how relevant each token in the context is to each other token
To identify and highlight key vocabulary terms in the front-end user interface layout
To compute probability values that decide when the generation loop must stop emitting tokens
questions.explanation: Attention computes weighted relationships between tokens, letting the model focus on the most relevant parts of the context when processing or generating each token.

questions.question: In self-attention, what roles do the Query, Key, and Value projections play?
options: Query encrypts the input parameters, Key decrypts them at execution, and Value stores the final results in memory
Queries are matched against Keys to score relevance, and those scores weight a sum of the Values
Query represents the user's prompt question, Key is the authorization token, and Value is the returned text response
They represent three identical copies of the weight matrix used to ensure structural hardware redundancy during inference
questions.explanation: Each token emits a query, key, and value vector. Dot products between queries and keys (scaled, then softmaxed) produce attention weights, which are used to take a weighted sum of value vectors.

questions.question: What is the softmax function's role in attention computation?
options: To dynamically adjust and soften the model's generated response tone when discussing highly sensitive or controversial user topics
To reduce overall model complexity by pruning redundant parameter matrices inside the multi-head attention layers
To implement dropouts by randomly cutting attention link connections to prevent model overfitting
To mathematically convert raw attention scores into a normalized probability distribution that sums to exactly 1
questions.explanation: Softmax converts raw query-key scores into normalized weights summing to 1, so each token distributes its attention proportionally across the context.

questions.question: Why do Transformers need positional encodings (or mechanisms like RoPE)?
options: Because content-based self-attention alone does not encode each token's absolute or relative position in the sequence
To identify and log the geographical GPS coordinates or server timezone parameters where the prompt text was originally compiled
To append structural weights that instruct the self-attention layer which tokens are most important
To implement compression matrices designed to pack long token sequences into shorter activation vectors
questions.explanation: Standard content-based self-attention does not identify token positions on its own. Positional embeddings or mechanisms such as RoPE add absolute or relative order information. A causal mask supplies a direction constraint, but it is not a complete substitute for positional information.

questions.question: Why does standard self-attention become expensive as sequences get long?
options: Because the language model weights must undergo custom alignment training for each new input sequence length
Because the attention mechanism computes pairwise scores between every token in the sequence, scaling quadratically with length
Because it forces input tokens to be processed in a strict sequential bottleneck with no potential for parallelism
Because the tokenizer vocabulary size increases dynamically in direct proportion to the input prompt length
questions.explanation: Every token attends to every other token, so compute and memory for the attention matrix grow as O(n²) in sequence length — a key driver of long-context research and optimizations like FlashAttention.

questions.question: What is a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture?
options: An ensemble configuration where several complete, distinct foundation models run in parallel to vote and achieve consensus on the final generated response
An enterprise operational pipeline where a certified panel of human domain experts manually reviews and validates all model generated outputs before release
A neural network design featuring multiple expert sub-layers, using a learned routing gate to direct each token to a subset of experts
A pre-training data pipeline method that dynamically mixes, filters, and samples text datasets from completely different academic vocabulary domains
questions.explanation: MoE models replace dense feed-forward layers with many 'expert' sub-networks and a learned router that sends each token to a small subset. This gives a large total parameter count while keeping per-token compute low; models like Mixtral and DeepSeek-V3 use this design.

questions.question: Which architectural pattern do most modern generative LLMs (like the GPT and Llama families) use?
options: Causally-masked encoder-only Transformer models
Autoregressive decoder-only Transformer models
Recurrent neural networks containing LSTM cells
Convolutional neural networks over pixel grids
questions.explanation: Most generative LLMs are decoder-only Transformers: a stack of causally-masked self-attention layers that predict the next token. Encoder-only models (like BERT) are used mainly for understanding/embedding tasks.
